stem: A permutation \( \sigma \) has a disjoint cycle decomposition consisting of one cycle of length [[a]] and one cycle of length [[b]] (acting on disjoint sets of points). What is the order of \( \sigma \) in the symmetric group?
solution: The order of a permutation is the least common multiple of its disjoint cycle lengths: \( \operatorname{lcm}([[a]], [[b]]) = \frac{[[a]] \cdot [[b]]}{\gcd([[a]],[[b]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the cycle lengths; the order is their lcm, not their product (unless they are coprime).
This adds the cycle lengths instead of taking the lcm.
This gives the gcd of the lengths rather than the lcm.
This uses only the first cycle's length.
This uses only the second cycle's length.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: A permutation \( \sigma \in S_{[[n]]} \) consists of a single cycle of length [[k]] together with [[n]] - [[k]] fixed points (\( \sigma \) leaves every other point unchanged). What is the order of \( \sigma \)?
solution: Fixed points are 1-cycles, which contribute a factor of 1 to the lcm and never affect the order. So the order of \( \sigma \) is just the length of its one nontrivial cycle: \( [[k]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the total number of points \( n \) for the order of \( \sigma \).
This uses the number of fixed points instead of the cycle length.
This multiplies unrelated quantities together.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This adds unrelated quantities together.

stem: A permutation \( \sigma \) has a disjoint cycle decomposition consisting of three cycles, of lengths [[a]], [[b]], and [[c]] (pairwise coprime). What is the order of \( \sigma \)?
solution: Since the three cycle lengths are pairwise coprime, their lcm is simply their product: \( \operatorname{lcm}([[a]],[[b]],[[c]]) = [[a]]\cdot[[b]]\cdot[[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the cycle lengths instead of taking their (product-equal-to-lcm) value.
This omits the third cycle length entirely.
This omits the first cycle length entirely.
This omits the second cycle length entirely.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: A permutation \( \sigma \) has a disjoint cycle decomposition consisting of TWO cycles, each of the SAME length [[a]] (acting on disjoint sets of points). What is the order of \( \sigma \)?
solution: The order is \( \operatorname{lcm}([[a]],[[a]]) = [[a]] \); repeating a cycle length does not increase the lcm beyond that single length.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly treats the two cycles as if their lengths were coprime and multiplies them together.
This wrongly adds the cycle appearances as if lcm scaled with the count of cycles.
This adds an unrelated constant instead of taking the lcm.
This wrongly halves the cycle length.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.
